title: Chapter 34: The First Seat's Gambit
content: The Seventh Seat arrived at Blackwood without warning.

Its constellation of lights materialized in the middle of Roxanne's office at midnight, casting strange shadows across the walls. Roxanne looked up from her sketches. Frost, standing in the corner, raised a crystalline hand defensively.
"You need to see this," the Seventh Seat said.
It extended a fragment of light. The light unfolded into a document — a scroll of compressed information, written in the language of the Primordials.
"What is it?" Roxanne asked.
"A petition. Circulated by the First Seat. It calls for a vote of no confidence in the Second Seat."
"Against me?"
"Against you. The First Seat claims that your audit was a personal attack, not a legitimate procedural action. It's asking the Council to strip you of voting rights pending an investigation."
"That's ridiculous."
"It doesn't have to be true. It only has to pass."
Roxanne read the document. The language was dense, layered with legal terminology from an era before human language existed. But the meaning was clear: the First Seat was fighting back.
"How many votes do they have?"
"The First, Third, Fourth, and Fifth Seats are in favor. That's four. They need four for a majority."
"The Sixth abstained last time."
"The Sixth is being pressured. The First Seat has offered to forgive several of the Sixth's outstanding debts in exchange for a favorable vote."
"Can they do that?"
"In the Council, everything is permitted. The rules are only as strong as the members' willingness to enforce them."
Roxanne set the document down.
"When is the vote?"
"Tomorrow. The First Seat moved quickly."
"Then I have one day."
"To do what?"
She picked up her black card.
"To remind the Sixth Seat why abstention is the safer choice."
The Sixth Seat's domain was a pocket dimension of perpetual twilight.

Roxanne stepped through a portal into a landscape of rolling hills covered in silver grass. The sky was a gradient of purple and grey, neither day nor night. A single structure stood at the center: a tower of black glass, spiraling into the mist.
"The Sixth Seat does not receive visitors," said a voice from the mist. It was soft. Female. Ancient.
"The Sixth Seat is about to lose their abstention advantage if they don't talk to me."
Silence.
Then the tower's door opened.

Inside, the tower was empty — hollow all the way up, with no floors or ceilings. Just a vertical shaft of space. At the center, a spiral of smoke rotated slowly. The same form Roxanne had seen in the Council chamber.
"You came all this way," the Sixth Seat said. Its voice echoed from everywhere at once. "To ask me not to vote against you."
"To ask you what the First Seat offered."
"Debt forgiveness. Forty thousand years of accumulated obligations. Wiped clean."
"That's substantial."
"It is freedom. From the oldest chains in existence."
"What would I have to offer to match that?"
The smoke spiral paused its rotation.
"What could you possibly offer?"
Roxanne held up the black card.
"I can offer you what the First Seat can't: a system where debt doesn't exist at all."
"You are offering me a philosophy. The First Seat offered me a transaction."

"Philosophies are just long-term transactions. The First Seat forgives your debts today. But tomorrow, you're still in the same system. Still dependent on their goodwill. Still vulnerable to their next negotiation."
"And your system?"
"My system is still being built. But it has one advantage: once it's built, you won't need the First Seat's permission to exist."
The smoke spiral was still.
"You are asking me to bet on a future that doesn't exist yet."
"I'm asking you to bet on a future that's better than a past you've been trapped in for forty thousand years."
A long pause.
Then the Sixth Seat spoke. "I will abstain again."
"Not vote with me?"
"I don't trust your future enough to vote for it. But I don't trust the First Seat enough to vote against you."
"Abstention is enough."
"It won't stop the no-confidence vote. Four votes are enough to pass it."
"I know. But abstention means you're not actively working against me. That's all I need for now."
Roxanne turned to leave.
At the doorway, she paused.
"One more thing."
"Yes?"
"The First Seat forgave your debts. But I can buy them back from the Exchange. The Frost-bringer is my accountant now. It knows where every debt is registered."
"You are threatening me."
"I'm reminding you that the First Seat's gift can be undone. My offer can't."
She stepped through the portal.
Behind her, the smoke spiral resumed its rotation — slower than before. Thoughtful.
